message.content: Adversaries may modify file or directory permissions on Linux and Mac systems to bypass access control lists (ACLs) and access protected files. This technique involves using commands like `chown` (change owner) and `chmod` (change mode) to alter permissions, potentially locking out legitimate users or making files executable. Such modifications can facilitate persistence, hijack execution flow, or enable other malicious activities. Notable adversaries like APT32, Bundlore, and TeamTNT have exploited this technique to execute payloads, modify key files, and evade detection. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and alerting on permission changes, employing strict ACL policies, and using file integrity monitoring tools to detect unauthorized modifications. Regular audits and employing the principle of least privilege can also help in reducing the risk of exploitation.
